Hey, it's Sean and Andre. It is week five of the Art of Email workshop. Today what we thought we would do was bookend the entire experience of the Art of Email. So in the introduction, we explained our four strategic upstream decisions. And then in just the nature of how, you know, we've certainly experienced this ourselves and nature of going through a course you're really excited to kind of get into it, see what it's all about. So we can imagine some people came through and had an experience where you went through the introductory material and I was like, okay, let's get to the next thing and the next thing and now what we'd like to do now that we're at the end and we have the vantage point of looking back over the totality of the Art of Email. We want to revisit those four strategic upstream decisions and we certainly encourage you to go back and read those, those modules because there's, we did not, we did not articulate those lightly and we did not come to those lightly. Started as a much longer list and we continue to hone it and to strip away the unessential and to combine things and what was left those four decisions really. There's a lot in there. So I think what we'll do is we'll just take them in order. So decision number one is emphasize relationships before transactions. So Andre, I'll hand that to you and we'll just ping pong back and forth and we'll just go through all of them. Really, it doesn't, I don't think it really matters where, what perspective we take, we can just share some thoughts about each of those. So I'll put you in the hot seat and let you start talking about relationships before transactions. Yeah, okay, so let me quickly hit the rewind button slightly and just to reiterate some of the things that you said leading up to our starting this and, you know, Sean and I have been looking at marketing more broadly and then within the context of our various course. Well, the various things that we do, which are codified in our various courses. So, you know, out of email is this lens in which we see the world and how we interact through email and interact with an audience. So this is not just an email marketing course. It is far more than that. And one of the reasons why I took took us so long to, to, to actually birth this thing is, we really had to wrestle with with some of our wall externalize some of our, our strategic upsteam decisions put names and and stuff behind it. And it was important for us to wrap this up in, as you sing it over here, that allowed us that that informed everything else inside of art of email in a way that we thought that we thought would add huge amount of clarity and and power to people that are, you know, on this journey and and don't necessarily see the world or haven't in these and as expressed in these boxes that then inform everything else in the course. But now that most of you, listening to this have been through our VML. When you revisit these, these, these, these four decisions. So I think re looking at these things is a really powerful way to just internalize them again to a point where eventually it'll be completely automatic and this weren't just sharp in in your email marketing efforts, it'll be everything like, you know, marketing more broadly and life in general, and this will just show up everywhere so yeah, I just, I just felt that it was important to frame that like that so our first idea is this is this is this main thing which is emphasize relationships before transactions and yeah so one of the ideas is, and this is this is not going to be a revelation to many people that it's easy to overvalue short term goals so that that that feeling that I have a prospect coming through this this this thing this this this final this email series and I need them to buy this thing to monetize, you know, to monetize them otherwise I'm going to lose them lose the attention and they're gone. It's far more difficult to think of the world in in long term, you know that if we if we just pause and think about this as a relationship and establishing relationships with every single person that that enters our world, you know, as a priority, and through the context of that. Everything else downstream becomes easier, certainly I'm selling selling just becomes, you know, 10x I just I'm just going to throw a number out there but it just becomes, it comes so much easier. So, but it's, it's more difficult to look at the world that way, and because there's this thing called we need to monetize attention as quickly as possible. So, that's one of the things we try to emphasize in this lesson which really shows up in the entire art of email and in fact everything everything we do. You know, until be and whatever so I think that's that's the first insight that we want to draw attention to. Before waffle on anything else you want you want to. I just want to touch on pack here. Yeah, I always have one of your when you were talking I was having kind of a trip down memory memory lane. You know, over two decades running an agency and one of the things that used to used to bother me a lot, and probably still does I'm just not as close to it but I've you know I've purchased hundreds of thousands of dollars of training over the years I said, I really enjoy learning more about my craft. And I always bothered me a lot is buying training that was, it always felt like it was partial, it was in or it was incomplete. And, you know, I certainly want you know I learned a lot from virtually everything that I purchased sometimes I learned how not to do things but in general, you know there was there was value in it but what bothered me was that because I because I ran an agency I needed to have. And then the next time you work with a new client. So I was, I was always looking for you know what was something I could stand on that just made sense for everything and what I would find a lot is now it's worth, you know, purchase a course on, you know, you name it. Any part of digital marketing that it often had the same format, which was, it was a thing that worked for somebody at a point in time in a certain way. Yes, sometimes it was, it could be replicated often it could be replicated but what was generally true is that it was, it was kind of a point in time type of context idea, and those are fine and we need those in our toolkit, sometimes they can be strategic sometimes they can be tactical. But what I, what I kept searching for was what's, what's below that, you know, and Elon Musk and others have, you know, popularize this idea of first principles and that what I was looking for like what are the, what are the first principles because once, once I could establish first principles, then every new situation I didn't have to start over so every new client that I worked with every new market I didn't, I didn't have to start over. So, over the years slash decades when I was, I made a very small number of things that I could, that I felt like I could stand on that were foundational. I mean, we were talking about the art of email. I think that's just part of my DNA now that I'm, I want things that are true. I want to know, like, when you, you know, for if you're going to open a grocery store somewhere. I'd like to know like, like how do, how do those businesses work, what are the, what are the main drivers for everywhere for a grocery store like it seems like those are questions that have been answered. And in our world, these questions have been answered. Sometimes they've been answered poorly, sometimes they've been answered in completely. But this is a long circuitous way to get back to this idea that emphasizing relationships before transactions is, is foundational truth. It's a first principle. It's, you know, this idea that and it doesn't matter what the percentages are. But what is seems to be true from all the data we've seen and there's lots of data available about this. This isn't us just looking at our experience and saying, Oh, this must be true for everyone. We're just looking at the experience of our, of our customers of people who are working with lots of clients, my own experience working with clients, our experience working with clients and we have lots of friends and colleagues who have occurred but there seems to be some truth, like capital T truth, that when we look at the source of buyers, most people who buy don't buy right away, and not like 5050 most people like 8020. Most people don't buy. And that just seems to be true and we can certainly look at our own experience and see evidence of that everywhere. When we get to something like that to me it feels like there's this idea of relationships before transactions is universally true, it get it rises to the level of gravity, which is the metaphor we've used elsewhere in this workshop and it's, there's something really powerful about that because, you know, and I think we're going to focus on a Q&A call or elsewhere and Andre and I have certainly sent it our conversation. When you jump out of an airplane gravity doesn't care if you believe in it or not, you're still racing to the ground. And if you don't have a parachute, you know, that's, it's, what's the expression you have the rest of your life to figure out how what's going to happen so relationships before transactions is it's just, it just recognizes the gravity of marketing that most people were going to buy from you. It's funny in the way that we expressed it because it's a, it's a decision and that's what makes these decisions so important. It's something that you can choose to do. It's a place where you have agency where you can make a choice, and that choice aligns you better with gravity. And once you do that, you're not fighting forces all the time, but if you have a high pressure sales funnel, and you're, you're really going after a tiny minority of the marketplace, then you have to fill that funnel all the time because you're only, you're only able to reach a tiny minority of the market. That's just, that's just truth. So when, when we look at that we say, wait a minute, what decision could we make that by making that decision, every decision that follows is better and emphasizing relationships before transactions, any dimension that you look at it, financial overall business performance experience you name it every dimension that you evaluate this decision, it's a better decision than the alternative. And to me that, that is a, that's something foundational that everybody can rest on. All right, I'll set up decision number two. I like this. I'll hand it off to you and it gives me time to think. And, you know, that's, that's not the point of this. I just wanted to mention that, you know, this isn't about a non transactional or business that doesn't create transactions, at least until, until way later. That's not the case. And it's a good point that you just made about, you know, or versus and it's, it tends to be presented this way, like, you know, you could, you can do, you know, traditional direct response, or you can focus on relationship building, and that's, it's a false choice, because behind the edge of those strategies is a reality, and the, the, you know, fast sale focused approach, a typical direct response approach. It all, I mean, yes, it works, of course it works, but it limits the size of the audience that you have to engage with by a factor of four or five. And again, we say this all the time you said every day you sit down to a buffet of consequences. So when you make that decision you've just limited the size of your audience, significantly and what we're suggesting is that, and then the way you said it is exactly right just choose both. Just, you know, yes, there are going to be people who come into your world, and they want to buy the thing when email number one arrives and there are situations where maybe it's, you know, a PS or a flitter or something. But we're not saying like don't you know, prevent people from buying we're in the, we're in the happy customer creation business, and some small percentage of happy customers are created in the first few days. So let them buy, but just never at the expense. Don't engineer the system to focus on those people because that's the, that's the tiny minority of people were eventually going to buy so if we have to, if we, if we need to emphasize one over the other. Or if we want to look at the whole picture, when we make this decision to focus on relationships, we're capturing the largest segment of the audience with our primary strategy, and not at the expense of the smaller subset who are going to buy anyway. All right, let's move on to the second one. This is this. You want to add something or you get. Good. And then decision number two. Decision number two is funny because we, we struggle struggle but we came at this from two different directions, and then we eventually put them together. So the first one is an observation and then the second one is a decision. So the observation is that customers are created before they buy anything. And then the decision really is just because of that treat everyone like a customer. So that's it. That one, that was an interesting one to create because we were really saying we were really sort of setting things up and then the decision and response, which again gets back to this first principle. It's like that it's, it's just true that customers are created. It's to think of a customer only as someone who has purchased misses the larger point that the buying decision is generally made before someone hands over their credit card. And because of that, because that's true, it's just, it's a very effective decision to say, you know what, I'm just going to treat everybody like a customer all the time. I'll, I'll actually, I'll introduce the three ease here and then I'll hand it off to you. These were not intended to be the three ease but I guess I looked at it as I go that made sense. So, you know, the three ease are really that when you treat everyone like a customer. It's easier, it's more efficient and it's more enjoyable. It's not intentionally three ease but it's easier and that you don't have to, you know, when we get an email from somebody and they're asking us questions, we don't pause and then go look up their, their records like do they own anything we don't it's, it's just easier just to say I'm just going to treat everybody like a cut like a good friend like a customer. Right, so it's easier in that sense it's more efficient. Again, because there's not this friction of, wait a minute, what do I tell somebody who is a customer to persuade or who isn't a customer so I can persuade them versus somebody who is. So it's, it's all of that adds friction so you eliminate that a freak at friction you just treat everyone the same way. And it is more enjoyable, you know, and enjoyable is subjective but it, it's at least from my perspective. It's, it's just, it's much more fun when someone asks a question that you don't try to figure out all the nuances of their context relative to us and it's just, you know, it's really nice to be able to look and see the question that's being asked, or questions that are being asked. And it's about how best what the value that we could give in response to that, and then do that without it that's just enjoyable, right it's it's just in the, in the spirit of interacting with other human beings, it's just more enjoyable. This decision I really love this decision because it solves so many potential downstream problems it eliminates them. And that's what, what's one of the beauties of a strategic upstream decision, isn't that it necessarily solves problems is it prevents them the problems never arise. And that's the best way. All right, I'll hand that off to you for some commentary. And when you, when you zoom out and you, when you really internalize these, these, these decisions and these these fundamental truths, as we present as we presented them over here, they all mesh together in in such a beautiful way. So this one in particular, I mean, you can observe this yourself by looking at your own buying behavior and I'm sure there's probably many more buying decisions you've made that happen way before money has ever been, you know, sent you know where you've at the moment. You know, in many cases this is happening, you know, this can happen days, weeks, months, even years prior to to actually going there and doing the transaction. And we've observed this so many times in our business, because we obviously always interacting with with people and we and we love seeing emails come in with people, people essentially stating this like I can't wait to buy stuff. I mean, normally that's that's on the roll up to a to to to some sort of PLS but even, even more broadly than that. You can see this playing out all the time and it's just such a such an amazing thing when you really understand what's going on. I have a tendency to because behavior, you know, because we're looking at the other side of it and we can see our email analytics and and dashboards and whatever and there's just so many things that happen that you can see that it's, you can put your finger on on on something and say this person's going to going to become a customer, become a customer because of how they interacting with us over email, or whatever and there's no, you know, no one's talking about buying anything. In many cases, it's just the questions that they are asking and how, you know, how invested they are in in trying to solve a problem that they have. And we having these these discussions over email and it's not this person has is about to make that decision and other people you can you can totally tell that they've, they made that decision already, and it's just a matter of when is right for them. Maybe something's closed maybe something's open but you know, everyone's context is different. But yeah when you really recognize that this happens. It is a very liberating thing. It allows you to, to create marketing that's very different to to the typical stuff you see out there. This is, I mean I'm hesitant to say this next part because it can be so easily misconstrued but I'll say it's not to provide the context but it's the statement is pretty clear which is you know people tend to act like customers when they're treated like customers. That's just, that's just true. Now, the way that can go wrong is it can sound like it's like sort of this Machiavellian tactical like well I just want to treat everybody like customers because I want them to pay me money and that's, that's not the conclusion to reach from this the decision is that everything is easier and better if you just treat everybody like customers. And now there certainly are people out there, and I probably some fairly credible arguments that you want to have this kind of walled garden that's attractive to people outside your who are not paying customers who they know there's something amazing on the other side of the garden wall so that it encourages them to become paying customers but guess what that happens anyway. Right, that's just if when you treat everybody like a customer and you go above and beyond and you mean it, then the implication is that whatever is on the other side of that walled garden is even better. And of course that's true. Right. And of course, yes, our courses are far more complete than the free material that we give out that's that's that's self evident, but it doesn't mean that we that we would treat people who are interested in that differently because they don't, they don't own the thing that's the, that's kind of you know, if that's, that's an illogical or that's that's an ear I guess that's an irrational decision to make is that well until you're willing to put your money on the table, I'm not willing to give you the highest level of XYZ my dad used to tell us a story about the difference between effort and output and that the story was of an old man who was sitting next to his wood stove, and he would say to the stove if you give me more heat, I'll give you more wood. And of course that's anyone who's ever said, front of the wood stove knows that's not how, how things work right so this idea of treating everyone like a customer really is recognizing that when we give to other people more we invest in other people more. And that's what people tend to respond to that. And yes, there are certainly going to be people who, you know, quote unquote, take advantage of that. They're certainly going to people who maximize, you know what they get for free for me, that's fine those people are out there anyway, that's that's going to happen anyway and they're not, they're not your customers. They're not going to be the people who buy your courses. That's just fact. So, it, you know on balance now if you spend all day with people like that and that's a challenge but my guess is you're not going to they're going to be one off here and there. But that's not how we're not investing in this and this gets back to the common run Andre made earlier about long term versus short term focus. If we narrow our focus to into too much of a short term. Time frame, we're like, oh man today I spent 20 minutes creating a screencast answer questions for someone and then they didn't buy. That automatic like that that's not the right timeframe to look at it, a better timeframe to look at is look over 90 days and evaluate how it feels to treat everybody like a customer over 90 days and on balance what effect does that have on your business that's a far better time frame to consider or a year as another one but when we focus to myopically. It's easy to see especially easy to see contrast, and we'll see the ones and we'll remember the ones because we seem to be hardwired to be to be more to focus more on the negative. That's why the news is so miserable. So if we have 10 interactions with people and nine of them eventually become paying customers and one doesn't will tend to remember the one who doesn't. So just don't pay attention to that right just just treat everybody like a customer like a friend like and you can I mean you can you can substitute friend to respond to the treat people like customers. Period. I mean that's it. You know we could have shortened decision number two to three words and wait is that for treat people like that's five words treat everyone like customers it's four words. And that that single decision eliminates infinite numbers of downstream problems and creates infinite amounts of downstream benefit. So that's why it rises to the level of being a strategic upstream decision, but it's irreducible it is. It is so powerful and that's it you know again getting back these four decisions are so powerful that when you make them. Everything changes, and it changes in ways that you can't, you can't predict you can expect we can predict in some ways but mostly it's, they're so powerful that you're just you're not you, it's hard to imagine a business on the other side of these decisions when you make them because they're so profound. That's why we put them at the beginning of the art of email because you make these decisions they inform what we said, and now that we're at the end when we look back and you can say okay now go back and look at all of the different types of things you can do with the art of email, but do that and think about these decisions now and you'll see why they show up everywhere and why they're so powerful. So I think you're, you're, go ahead and run your favorite one. So, you know, our, our emerging marketing newsletter. We have one emerging marketing newsletter that goes to everyone. We don't have emergency marketing emerging marketing newsletter that goes out to all prospects. We have the gold plated edition that goes out to customers of one course, and we have the platinum version that goes out to customers of multiple courses, where we write three newsletters every, every two weeks and we try and figure out what's the best stuff we can share with the platinum guys. What's, what can we take away for the gold plated guys and what can we remove for the, for the prospect people. That is, that makes no sense in the world to us and yet it's it's decisions like that. I mean, it's, it's, it's thinking like that, that shows up all over the marketing world, you see it everywhere. So, you know, we're writing our, our newsletter is it's, what is the most value we can share in a newsletter that's, you know, one and a half thousand to 2000 words, you know, what is the most we can pack into that that's going to be valuable to every single person that receives this thing, which is a completely out of way of looking at it. It's, it's nothing about what can we remove, you know, the, what can we remove doesn't exist. It's what can we put in within the constraints of the length that we're going to be sharing. That's going to be most impactful for, well, everyone. It's interesting to think about like, it would actually, you know, it would probably go the opposite direction. If we really wanted to do this well. And if we really believe this idea of three levels of newsletter made sense, it wouldn't be, we wouldn't be giving more to each audience, we would probably be taking away from me at each level. So it's that was it Linus Pauling whoever said, you know, if I had more time, I would have written shorter letter. Right. So it's like, you know, you write, you write a, you know, you can imagine writing an email and then, you know, hacking away at the unessential in Bruce Lee's where it's stripping it down. And by the time you get to the, you know, the platinum version, it has like four words on it. Right. You're like, this is this week is these four words. But it's what you just said it's, it's this, you know, not to get too intellectual here, but this, this points to something that's really fascinating and it happens a lot. I'm sure it happens everywhere, but we're familiar where it happens in our world, which is something is emergent. And then it gets turned into something deliberate. And when that happens, something is lost. So the, the continual ascension of a small minority of people in your audience to higher and higher levels is an emergent property of large audiences. And it just, it is what it is. It's a byproduct of the Pareto principle. It seems to be a power law that shows up everywhere. I mean, even shows up, I was surprised to hear this the other day, which I hadn't thought about that even the size of planets and solar systems and other things seems to follow a Pareto distribution that, you know, some small minority of planets, have the largest responsible for the most mass, like, it's interesting. So in any large audience, in a large audience is probably, you know, what it really starts to get the Pareto principle starts to show really an audience is like 1000 and in scales upward from them from there. But if you think about, let's just say for the sense of art, say, the sake of argument that we have an audience of approximately 10,000 people, the preponderance of people in that audience aren't going to buy anything. Right, that's, that's just sort of reality. Some significant percentage of that audience will buy something, you know, some small price point. And then, and you can there's, there's just a distribution that's what a Pareto distribution is. It's an asymmetrical distribution, meaning that there are lots of people who are in your audience who will spend a little bit of money, and a few people who will spend more money and then a tiny minority who will spend a lot of money. I think the example that Richard Cosh uses is Starbucks that Cosh is the, has written a ton about 80 20. You know, in a typical Starbucks, the lowest price item that you can buy is a $2 and 60 cents cup six $2 and 60 cent cup of coffee, and the fully stocked Starbucks also has I believe it's a $1,500 espresso machine. That doesn't seem to make sense, but it is a classic Pareto distribution that when you are when you put thousands of customers through a store all the time. Most of them will buy something in the $5 range. That's where most of your action happens. But in a large audience, there is generally there are a tiny number of people who will buy the $1,500 espresso machine like that's, that's, that's just what the Pareto principle says so getting back to the original point. And the fact that you might have small groups of people who are willing to pay you more and more money for what from there, what they perceive as more value. That's just an emergent property of any large audience period. What goes wrong is when we then try to make that deliberate. So what you see happen in the, the internet marketing world a lot is people build up audiences and then they create a gold membership, right and then after that they have to create a platinum membership. And then eventually it's titanium and then you know, stardust or whatever, like, just keep ascending and ascending and then the group gets smaller and smaller and pays more and more. Right and that, that makes sense. There's nothing wrong with an ascension model like that because in a large audience you're going to have people who are raising their hand who want more, and that more is valuable to them. That's just, that's fact. But where it goes wrong is, is when we then make that deliberate. And we say, oh, okay, well if that's true then I'm going to start treating my audience differently. I'm going to give most of them the free stuff. And then we give another group the better stuff, and then another group and it's like that's not when we make it deliberate we blow things up. And the better way to do it is to, to let it be emergent, and then interact with what emerges with those people specifically. And that's sort of an intellectual detour but don't, this happens all the time, like something, and it feels like it's unique to internet marketing I'm sure it's not. It's like, there's observations are made and then, then everything gets broken because people you know that an emergent observation tries to be made deliberate so that can be monetized and the whole thing falls apart. I'm going to be quiet now we'll move on to the third one I think this is your favorite actually I think, I think relationships before transactions is your favorite but this is I think close, close second so the decision is, you know the the observation is, is worth the power of world building and this decision is to build worlds for your audience to inhabit. That's really when you make that decision. Lots of things happen. I'll make one observation and then I'll hand it over to you or just ping pond back and forth so the real this gets back to this idea of gravity and being honest about what reality is and reality is that the real world is non linear, and it's messy. We can wish that's not true. We can create funnels that that don't have links to anything else, and so that we feel like we can then direct traffic into those funnels, and that we feel like we have some sense of control over the experience with the end user. And then when we look at our analytics and we see that a leads to be leads to see leads to D that we can pat ourselves on the back when we think we're optimizing things we can do all of that. But that does not change the fact that the world is non linear, and that a tremendous number of people are going to start going into a funnel and then leave and maybe go search the internet to find out, you know, your name plus review your name plus scam, like to figure out or you know they're trying to figure out the things you're not telling them, or someone might be on, you know, checking out the grocery store and see a link to something that you're selling and click on it. And in that frame of mind they're not interested at all but something peaks their curiosity. And then two weeks later, something shows up in Facebook that reminds them of that thing that has nothing to do with you. But then they go back and they search through their email and they try to find it then they find it, and then they go back and when you sit down and you look at your analytics you see this beautiful false causality that a leads to be leads to see and you pat yourself on the back we've all done this about how you know how smart your decisions are because of all the data you have and reality is, most of it is complete nonsense. It's mostly just fiction that we've that is, you know, it is what I say fabricated I don't mean deliberately, but it's kind of a collective delusion that we've all agreed to that somehow we can make it nonlinear and then improve it in a linear way and that's just not how the world works 99% of the time or more. Alright, we're building over to you and then I'll make a couple more things I'm sure you ever about in a minute. Yeah, I do love world, world building it seems so so intuitive but then I, you know, I wasn't recognized that whenever I start talking about world building to people and the glaze over it is probably not intuitive to the large majority of people. So, one of the things I used to use this analytics tool, I think it's still around but it, it hasn't changed. I don't know if you've ever heard of it before. But I used it for for many, many, many years. And one of the features inside of it is you could you could look at the real time. So you have like a, like a little map of of your websites, the content, the pages that the people are interacting with. And it's got a map of the world. And you can see people from around the world like it shows you little dots of them interacting with with the site and then on on what pages that they've just landed on. And I found this so fascinating I would just stare at my love view like an idiot. And one of the big insights was that on a website with hundreds of pages. There were little stars, you know, people interacting with like landing on pages, like all over the place. And so there was no single point point in. You know, this is this is, you know, websites, most websites don't have even if you think you have a linear funnel, people probably aren't 100% of people aren't starting on on page one. But just just viewing this was was so opening to me and even though there was, you know, there's multi pages on on, you know, within the world. And then you can see most people interacting on on other pages and then you can just see how they how they move along and somebody will like land on a page and move along, and then bounce somewhere else and their little little dot shows up somewhere else. And that's just a very visual representation of how nonlinear reality really is. And even though they are linear parts on a website and you know some web parts websites may only have a linear path and that's it. That was an amazing insight that that really validated more than anything this idea to me. So yeah, that's that's well building and out of the sea and out of the purchases are made in the last two weeks. And we got it. We upgraded the order ring. I ordered something called super sapiens it's a it's a CGM ordered an iPhone 13 pro and then I was uncertain whether I should go for the pro or the pro max to pro loans and enrolled in two fiction writing courses and one of those purchases and can somebody who owns those those websites look at their analytics and and tie that back to me in some meaningful way that would that would have that would have the context that would allow them to make some decision that would be meaningful. It's not going to happen. My decisions were so messy and came, you know, I became a customer for all of these ones every single one of them way before I actually made the made the purchase and there was an element of world building going on here in the sense that I was doing my research and I was bouncing around and I was reading and I was interacting certainly on one of these websites the fiction writing one it's it's it's a big site. So I spent a lot of time on there to try and figure out which which course was the most relevant for me and I was looking at you know some videos and and I ended up in running into and that process was, you know, that was world building that that was playing out on those various internet properties. So yeah, I think do you have any anything else to add to that. I mean I think this idea of an easy place to start you just did it. So you open the door which is think about the last dozen purchases made and ask yourself. Don't focus on laundry detergent. You know it's not those kind of purchase but also don't it's not because I have to be a new car either. But when we make decisions, and when we make purchases, and this is you know Clayton Christiansen's work on jobs to be done theory starts to touch on this a little bit and you know you can go look at the audience and offer master class will we broke that down. Because people, first of all people buy for a lot of different reasons, which is is one type of context, right could be a functional reason I just need to get things done, could be a social emotional like there are lots of reasons, people buy value signaling is a reason many people don't buy value signaling, not, you know, no judgment, sure we all do it. But there are, I mean, they're, it's an in buying is infinitely complex. And just because we decide to make buying the buying process, more linear doesn't mean we actually make buying decision making more linear. It's just not like we can it's, I just, you know, I don't know why this, this image is in my mind but it's like the toddler on the floor, you know banging, you know banging is this on the floor demanding that people buy differently. It's like, no, I mean react to the way people buy in the way, and I think the reason this is such an issue was that was, we people want to can, you know, we want to have a sense of agency in the sense of control, particularly when you, if you're spending a lot of money to acquire customers, then there are then all of a sudden this need to have really something that makes you less fearful about that spend becomes really important. So I get it. I understand why people, you know, I understand why funnels are engineered, I understand why we are all subject to this collective delusion that we can make a nonlinear messy world feel more linear and we probably can make it a little more linear for a small percentage of people. But the moment you take a step back and you think to yourself, let me think about some things I've purchased and how like what was involved in that purchase and one of the examples I gave it, you know, great detriment embarrassment to myself in the audience and offer masterclass was the suit for military all black watch, right? Like, that's, if you look at that buying experience, it was the most ridiculous experience from a like, analytic perspective, because there was absolutely no first I'm not a watch guy, there's, it was $300 watch which I never spent $300 and a watch before. I didn't, I can't read the damn thing because it's like it's gray. It's like a gray numbers on a black, all black watch. It's so it's difficult to see and all the functionality it has. I don't, like, I don't use it, you know, has an altimeter and it has all kinds of I don't use it I don't need it. But that was the watch that was in the movie The Equalizer. Right. And when I saw it, I was like, damn, what that watch is badass right. Why I don't know why I know and I didn't, I didn't get you know I didn't go online that day and order it. Right. A lot of people did it was sold out like for, I don't know 18 months after the movie was sold out their websites dedicated to it. Like it's crazy. But then I happened to be in a store, a brick and mortar store and they had one. And I bought it. And when you look at that I mean that to me that's like it's such a ridiculous example. But when, when that like for that purchase, it's to me is such a microcosm that the, so the person who owns the brick and mortar store could you know try to decipher like oh did you see our flyer we sent out this week and it was like no, nothing to do with your advertising like there was no way to explain that and that kind of I think that's how most people are traversing the world when they buy stuff they might, they might be become aware of you at one point and this is this is why we get back this idea of relationships before transactions as well, that these all interrelate because when you establish relationships with somebody particularly relationships that are that emphasize empathy first, credibility and authority later. Like we all want to know the person to go to for in a certain area of our life, right, we want to know that person. And when you emphasize relationships you build worlds around these areas of expertise that you have an experience, you become the person who represents the solution, when somebody decides that they want that solution. That's just kind of how the world works. Now you might be one of several. By emphasizing these relationships and by building worlds with with content and particularly content in the digital sense. When you do that, you you stack the deck so much in your favor that when someone is ready to buy that they've already in their mind positioned you as the person to buy from. This is how the all these things interrelate. And when we try and this gets back to this idea of emergent versus deliberate when when we try to be deliberate about making the buying system versus recognizing that for most people buying is emergent, it happens when it's right for them. It could be, you know, there could be a situation where they think they want it now they choose not to buy now their life context changes six months later, something else happens, they see something and then they come back and they buy at that point and we can't. We can't engineer a linear system to do that. So, this idea of building worlds that position you as an empathetic credible authority, and emphasize in the relationship with your audience, before trying to make any sale and engineering any deliberate way to extract money from them, and then treating everybody like you're already a customer, you start to see how these things all interrelate. And what we've described as four things really are for their four lenses on the same, like central idea that we just don't have a name for. These are approximations of the name. That's something about hey this is how the world works. So work like the way the world works. Alright, and one of the last one or you want to add anything to the world building. Yeah, let me add one more thing and actually just to just to turn to this thing that doesn't have a name and you know there's there's a reason why our, our newsletter is called emerging marketing right. You know, much of what we do is emergent and you know it just happens if when you when you do things in in this way. So, you know, there's an argument against world building. And if you if you really trying to hear if you heard what we said where that buying decisions are very nonlinear, and people will bounce around and then they'll just make a buying decision and and then make a purchase. And that seems like there's, there's no agency from from our perspective. Someone's just going to land on our website and either going to buy or they're not. And that's not the case, you know, Sean Sean drew attention to the fact that if you have lots of content on a site. You stacking the deck in your favor from from multiple perspectives, you know, one of which is is Google, you know, we can't get around the fact that you know Google ranks websites and it's it's it's lens on what's what's trustworthy and what's more trustworthy, you know, in its ranking algorithms is all tied down to the, you know, content and if that content is valuable people are going to link to it blah blah blah. So, having that there that's around your world and people are are essentially living like they've they've made a home on your website for example, you know, that's a that's a huge signal. As opposed to a website that's just a single sales that you know if you want to look at the extremes. So when I, for example, when I when I bought these when I enrolled in these two writing courses, I started doing doing web searches. And I just ended up on this site in particular and many others. But this one was just there was there was more there there was there was something that I could sink my teeth into I could see things that made sense. And I interacted, and I spent lots of time many hours interacting with the website watching some videos and reading because it's you know it's for artists, so there's so there's lots of long form content. And it was completely different to many of the other sites that were really more trying to force force a sale here you know he has he has my thing sign up for my webinar. And I know what's going to happen that's not what I wanted to interact with. So there is there's huge amount of value in in building out your world slowly but surely over time to a point where people want to essentially live on this, you know, live in the space and inhabit it so that's my that's my final, my final thoughts on will build. And I'll just add one last sentence here which I think about a lot when if when I'm working on content or writing email whatever it is. I think I remind myself a lot is that everything happens in context, we know very little about every visitor who comes to your website, every person who reads your email, every person who checks out your Facebook page, your YouTube channel, whatever it is. They are having an internal dialogue, and they're subject to contacts that we have very, very little insight into. And once you recognize that you stop trying to you stop trying to play God essentially, right, it's really it's easy to feel like with all this technology we have on our side and for marketing that we can, we can engineer an experience for somebody and play God to their experience but the reality is we can't that we can certainly engineer experiences but they're still just part of a much longer, much larger experience that we know very little about. And to me for some reason that's just freeing right but it just that, and I'm a practicing stoic which means that, you know, when you boil stoicism down to its bare essentials, the essence of stoicism is to focus your energy and attention on things over which you have control and to let everything else go. So I don't, I don't have control over the internal dialogue somebody is having when they come, when they, when they interact with our material I don't, I don't have control over their context, their context at all. But I do have control over what value I'm able to contribute, and I have some influence over the types of value that we co create, and how we share that value. So, it's just it's very freeing to just, I mean, yes, we still think about our audience we ask ourselves questions. Yeah, how, you know, how could this be value who do we think this could be valuable for. But it's that's different than trying to to figure out the unfigure out able. It's just easier to say this is this is how I want to show up in the world, I think this is valuable, or it's interesting or it's entertaining for my audience, and I'm going to do that, and trust that when I do that consistently that in measured over the appropriate time frames like 90 days to three to a year that hours and days that when I do that, my business is going to grow, my experience of that is going to be better, like everything's going to move in the right direction that's why these decisions are so powerful. All right, that brings us to the last one decision number for permission before promotion. Have you answered this one first and then I'll take a shot at it I love this one I think this might be my favorite. Yeah, this is, you know, this one is fundamental to to everything we do. In fact, every single person. Any of our courses have been subject to this multiple times all the time. Everyone's seen it nobody hasn't seen it. If, if they're in our world. I've tried to I've tried to think back to when when the started for me and I've not been able to get there. There was a point in 2006 that was very memorable for me because it was my, I did something that that shocked me in a way that it was my biggest promotion and and as a result my my biggest earning month. Ever and in that moment there was so many revelations and when I try to unpack or unpassable what I've done. This was one of the things but I can't remember before that so this may I may have done versions of this before then I don't know but it's this idea that you know we have an audience and just because we we have the permission of an audience to you know they've they've raised their hand to be on our email list and you know that's that's a fundamental thing you know we we we ask for permission. It's that doesn't mean that we always have permission to to expose them to stuff to promotions. You know, being relevant is a superpower and it's very, you know, it's an honor to be relevant but it's it is very very hard to be relevant all the time. Because you know people's interests change. I mean, you know people on an email list for for some broad reason, but that doesn't mean that they're going to be interested in every single promotion you've got so this is a mechanism that you can use to be respectful of people's attention. And the fact that you know 100% of people are never going to be interested in any one thing at any point in time. And for for reasons, you know, environmental other reasons that are that's just, you know, it's gravity, it's, it's reality. So this is recognizing that and then every single time there's a permission. I mean, there is a promotion. It's asking for permission and it does. It doesn't mind. It does a bunch of really powerful things when you ask for permission. So maybe we can, you know, I don't want to race ahead and ramble on about this. So if you want to interject before continue down the rabbit hole. And you're talking I was thinking about this is you know this is my nature to go back and to try to figure out why somebody. First of all, why would somebody choose permission before promotion and then why would the overwhelming majority of people choose the opposite. That's the thing that fascinates me because it is a case that's made. This is this is the case that's been made since really day one of email marketing is that whether you send up essentially sending emails is quote unquote free right you send you know 100 emails a week for most service providers email service providers or 10,000. The idea has always been there's no incremental costs so why not send more emails to more people. And then, but this is where it's such a. It's such a false choice because it's not. It's looking at one dimension of cost and not thinking about all the dimensions of cost. So there's the, when we, and so the, this is where I just find this where my rational mind takes over and makes me like, from righteous indignation where I'm like what that's that's such a short sighted thing to do is to say what doesn't cost you the sender more money to send more emails, and in general, when you send emails people will buy, therefore, you should send more emails, or just, and then when it gets more nuanced, you have people say well, you know, of course you send every promotion to everybody it doesn't cost you anymore and you know when you send more emails you get more sales. Okay. So, one dimension of cost. That's not all the dimensions of cost. So, when we, when we, first of all, we can we can look at this two different two additional ways one, what is the cost to the customer, or what is the cost to the recipient, because they're paying with the only resource they have that's finite, the most precious resource your audience has is their attention. So, immediately, we see there's a there's a tremendous asymmetry and costs like yeah I can send people emails all day every day. But the thing that they need to consume my emails with is the only thing they can't get more of, which is time and attention. So there's tremendous cost to them. And then when you take a step downstream from that, and you say okay well what are the consequences. And then, to me, and to them. If I continue to send them things that are not relevant to them at this moment. Well, it's a natural human response to pay less and less attention to things that we're less and less interested in, when demands are made of our attention all the time. So, other things have to happen we have to shout louder to get people's attention because you know we're shouting all day every day. So, when we really need them to pay attention to something we have to shout louder like all of these things happen. They're all downstream consequences of this one really bad decision, which is just to assume that there's no cost. If we send everybody everything all the time. And it's so easily disproven that that's not true. What I think the reason for me that permission before promotion is is at the core of what makes the art of emails so unique is that it. It solves so many problems without creating any new problems. I mean think about that. How often we have to, you know, my least favorite word in the English language is compromise, which sounds like I'm just a miserable asshole but it's not because it's not that I don't want other people to get what they want. It's that I don't want other people to get what they want at the expense of what I want. Right, so I'm not looking for compromise I'm looking for solutions where the other person gets exactly what he or she wants, and I get exactly what I want to that to me is that I'm not inspired by the gold wrap to work in theory of constraints. But it's a powerful idea so if we ask ourselves, how could my audience just we'll just for the sake of our even say we have an audience of 10,000 people. How can my audience get exactly what they want from me all the time. And I can get what I want from my audience, all the time. Well what would that solution look like well that would be a solution where I could almost like I could if I could interview in a perfect world every single person and say hey this is this thing that's about to happen. Where are you at right now and they're like, oh I'm face down at work at these projects things don't have any time for great I'll take you off the list. Right, you could imagine doing that with 10,000 people doesn't scale well but you could imagine those conversations on a smaller scale, because most people at any given point in time are not interested in something that you are selling. That's just truth. So I if if I recognize that I haven't lost any opportunity, because those people aren't going to pay attention anyway. The moment we introduce this idea of permission before promotion. We get. And I think this is why it's such a powerful upstream decision, everything that follows as a result is you is uniquely positive. And that's such a rare thing maybe that's why it gets a lit up by this decision because it's so rare to find something that doesn't have costs, like you're not paying some price in exchange for a larger benefit. This is a decision where the recipient benefits, because we're respecting their attention, or we're respecting their inattention, and we're rewarding and respecting their attention. At the same time, we're not compromising our results because the people who are not going to pay attention are not going to pay attention anyway, so we're not losing anything. And you know the critique of this is that there's someone listening to this or someone like this and say oh that's not true because you know if you send out a bridge email and people aren't paying attention and then they find out later, and they would have purchased or maybe someone didn't think it was right for them but because you're so good at selling that you, you know you could convince an extra one or two percent of people to purchase. That's a red herring or I think it's a straw man because you're basically saying that in exchange for an incremental gain here and there, once in a while, I might get an extra 1% of sales if I really amp it up. The cost for doing that is that I train my audience to stop paying attention to me a lot. That's a terrible trade. So what this decision to me, you can tell I'm just so animated, I love this decision. I think the moment somebody wraps their head around this decision, it's a shockwave in their marketing because it takes away fear, right? Like if the people are going to pay attention, they're going to pay attention. Respecting that, like you don't have to be fearful. Like oh I have to send everything to everybody all the time or they won't buy, you don't. And when that fear goes away, like what emerges in the place of that fear is power, it's personal power. I would say it's a recognition that marketing can be extraordinary and it doesn't have to hit everybody in the face 24, 7, 365 like a sledgehammer. It can be a scalpel or a laser or whatever you want to think for a metaphor and it can be even better when we do that. This decision opens up that possibility and I absolutely, absolutely love this. So it's, we've got an hour and eight minutes already so I'm going to hand it over to you to wrap up decision number four and I'm going to then do my best to keep my mouth shut because I love this decision so much. Actually we haven't even engineered it this way but the week five review is somebody that's done a bridge email for the very first time in a world where they've just never done it. They've always done it the typical way of just everybody gets every email, everybody gets every promotion beginning to end. So it'll be worth watching that review because we're going to be recording that next and this is it and their metrics have gone through the roof so that's interesting. But yeah, I think my final word on this is, you know, when you really frame something in a powerful way, which is, you know, which is the whole idea behind it. Behind bridge emails. And then you frame that click and you really asking people is this something that you want to pay attention to for X long, you know, this is going to last a week, this is going to last two weeks, whatever that is or three days. If you're interested in this thing and it's been framed in a way that's that's broadly valuable to everybody on the list if everybody on the list is getting that bridge email. So it's not an interruption it's somebody where you can quickly, you know, think you know what but see that this isn't for them at this point in time. But the way that the email is written and that framing is still interesting and it's valuable. That doesn't take something away from them. They've seen something and then they've seen that you respectful enough that you asking people to raise their hand and they just don't click and they, you know, and they're thinking Well, that's cool. And then those people that do it's it's really focusing in their attention and they're and it's amping up their excitement about this thing that's about to happen, which doesn't exist when it's just done in the other way, where it's just like everybody gets everything, whatever anything goes out. And you kind of got to catch people in this in this weird email, new stream of stuff happening all the time it's how do you pay attention to that I mean nobody can pay attention to the, to every social stream that they that they're a part of, including email which email for for most into the market is look, I'm starting to go into rent now. Email for for most into the market is, is like a social stream it's, it's a Facebook news feed or Twitter news feed but it's, it's, it's powered by email it's, it's terrible. And so, yeah, so, you know, asking people for permission. This is the whole idea about this, it gets them excited about something that they are that they want to hear about that they want to help you know, if it's something tied to them, solving a problem, you know, it's, it's going to be extra, extra valuable, it's going to create some some sort of tension in them, and they're going to want to see where this goes. So, I think that'll be. Yeah, I'm rents over and that's my, that's my last word. Usually the one who rants. So I think I'll wrap up today with just a suggestion for everybody listening to this which is, as we saw, we're assuming that when you went through the art of email you went through it in a linear way you read, you know, beginning to end. If you haven't done that once. We encourage you to do that. You don't, you can skip chapter of the module 11 with the advanced ideas if you want to. For now, I mean those are, we call them advanced for a reason, right so if you don't feel like you need those yet and those will be added to over time. If you haven't done that yet please do that. Just go beginning to end. Take it all in. When you get to putting it all together in module 10 then there's a way to approach how to then implement it and many of you in this workshop have been implementing so we get it that you may have, you may have stopped at a certain place, done the work that's great. The reason I'm mentioning this is that there, there's a real opportunity once you've gone through the art of email to go back again and reread the strategic upstream decisions, because you will see them with new eyes. And that's, and really every time you do this, and this is a course that's meant to be, it's meant to be a couple of things one, it's a beginning to end course. Where you can go back when you do your next PLS and you can reread the PLS section. It's also an emergent course where the more you go through it, the more you will see the nuances of it that are not necessarily visible the first one to 10 times through at the, certainly at the module level and at the higher systemic level. But bookending your experience of the art of email the first time with the strategic upstream decisions. I think is it's a very valuable use of your time because these decisions don't, they're not just about email they're about marketing period. And when you see them with, when you saw them with the, with fresh eyes. I first went through it with the excitement of why this course is being released and I'm going to go through it and I get it now I'm excited to go. You were inclining forward a little bit, I'm sure, like all of us would be. And going through that and you had a certain effect. But now that you're on the other side of the course material revisiting those strategic upstream decisions will have a different effect you will see it with new eyes new, a new perspective, and more nuances available to you so I highly encourage everyone to do that.
